coredata.dc:title: Characterization of sensitized solar cells based on nanocomposites of protoporphyrin IX and microwave reduced graphene oxide quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Lopez-Picazo, P.I.
Sanchez-Tizapa, M.
Tostado-Plascencia, M.M.
Flores-Martínez, M.
Carreón-Álvarez, M.A.
Castañeda-Valderrama, R.
coredata.dc:description:
In this research we are reporting for the first time the elaboration y characterization of titanium dioxide sensitized solar cells based on nanocomposites of protoporphyrin IX and microwave reduced graphene oxide quantum dots. Reduced graphene oxide quantum dots were synthesized by microwave assisted cutting of reduced graphene oxide and mixed with protoporphyrin IX previous to be drop-casted on titanium dioxide. Characterization was done by dynamic light scattering, ultraviolet visible spectroscopy, Raman spectroscopy, atomic force microscopy, field electron scanning electron microscopy, impedance spectroscopy and current voltage measurements. There were blue and red shifts of Raman and absorption bands of protoporphyrin+reduced graphene oxide quantum dots nanocomposites, evidencing strong interactions because of π − π electrons stacking, this fact was confirmed by microscopy images showing full coverage of protoporphyrin surface by reduced graphene oxide quantum dots. Electrical characterization of titanium dioxide+protoporphyrin+reduced graphene oxide quantum dots showed one of the highest photo-responses and lowest resistivities, in line with impedance measurements in which these composites presented up to ∼ 200% increase in capacitance, versus
TiO 2
alone. Efficiency of the optimized cell was 6.13 %, this was a value 9.4 % higher versus titanium dioxide+protoporphyrin cell. The reasons for the behavior of optimized cells are discussed based on a bands diagram showing the possibility of a rectifier effect and because of the strong affinity between protoporphyrin and reduced graphene oxide quantum dots.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Titanium dioxide
Protoporphyrin
Dye sensitized solar cell
Reduced graphene oxide
Reduced graphene quantum dots

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of titanium dioxide by sol-gel
Synthesis of graphene oxide and reduced graphene oxide
Synthesis of reduced graphene oxide quantum dots by microwave assisted cutting
Reduced graphene oxide quantum dots and protoporphyrin IX
Reduced graphene oxide quantum dots combined with protoporphyrin IX

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Instituto Tecnológico y de Estudios Superiores de Occidente, Universidad Jesuita de Guadalajara
Consejo Nacional de Ciencia y Tecnología

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: M. Sanchez-Tizapa reports financial support was provided by University of Guadalajara. P.I. Lopez-Picazo reports a relationship with Consejo Nacional de Humanidades, Ciencia y Tecnologia de México, CONACYT that includes: scholarship for Ph.D studies.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Configuration for photo-response measurement on materials deposited on insulating glass.
Setup for impedance measurements.
Process of fabrication of a sensitized cell.
Ultraviolet visible spectroscopy of graphite, GO and RGO.
Ultraviolet visible spectroscopy of powders of RGO-QD, PIX and both materials combined.
Ultraviolet visible spectroscopy of photo-electrodes.

Photoluminiscence spectroscopy of RGO-QD.
Cuvettes containing water and RGO-QD. a) under visible light, b) under ultraviolet light.
Raman analysis of graphite, GO and RGO.
Raman analysis of RGO-QD as synthesized (RGO-QD + Ethanol) and purified (RGO-QD).
Raman analysis of RGO-QD at 633 and 780 nm.
Raman analysis of the combination of RGO-QD with PIX.
Raman analysis of photo-electrodes.
Atomic force microscopy of RGO-QD, a) topography, b) phase contrast, c) histogram, d) and e) profilometry of a smaller RGO-QD.
Dynamic light scattering of RGO-QD.
FESEM images of GO a) and b), c) RGO, d) RGO-QD, e) histogram of RGO-QD.

FESEM of
TiO 2
nanoparticles, a) image, b) histogram.
FESEM of PIX + RGO-QD a) PIX powder, b) PIX dissolved in N,N-DMF and drop-casted, c) zoom in figure b), d) PIX + RGO-QD.

FESEM of
TiO 2
+PIX, a) and b)
TiO 2
+PIX, c) zoom in figure b).

FESEM of compound materials, a) and b)
TiO 2
+PIX + RGO-QD, c) zoom in figure b). Particles numbered are: 1 RGO-QD, 2
TiO 2
nanoparticles.
Nyquist plots of the principal materials.
Photoresponse of photo-electrode materials.
J-V plots of sensitized cells.
Electronic exchange between PIX and RGO-QD.

Bands diagram of the
TiO 2
+RGO-QD + PIX system.
Solutions for drop-casting for photo-response measurements.

row.entry:
0.1 g
TiO 2
+ 3 mL PIX + 3.5 mL RGO-QD

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Density of defects in graphite, GO and RGO.

Values obtained from equivalent circuit fitting. Increases in capacitance are versus the capacitance of
TiO 2
.

Dark and light resistivities of materials.
Photovoltaic parameters of sensitized cells.

head.ce:title: Characterization of sensitized solar cells based on nanocomposites of protoporphyrin IX and microwave reduced graphene oxide quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Carretera Guadalajara-Ameca, Km 45.5, 46600 Ameca, Jalisco, Mexico
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Carretera Guadalajara-Ameca, Km 45.5, C.P. 46600, Ameca, Jalisco, México

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Blvd. Marcelino García Barragán 1421, esq. Calzada Olímpica, 44430 Guadalajara, Jalisco, Mexico

sa:affiliation.sa:address-line: Blvd. Marcelino García Barragán 1421, esq. Calzada Olímpica

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Blvd. Marcelino García Barragán 1421, esq. Calzada Olímpica, C.P. 44430, Guadalajara, Jalisco, Mexico

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
In this research we are reporting for the first time the elaboration y characterization of titanium dioxide sensitized solar cells based on nanocomposites of protoporphyrin IX and microwave reduced graphene oxide quantum dots. Reduced graphene oxide quantum dots were synthesized by microwave assisted cutting of reduced graphene oxide and mixed with protoporphyrin IX previous to be drop-casted on titanium dioxide. Characterization was done by dynamic light scattering, ultraviolet visible spectroscopy, Raman spectroscopy, atomic force microscopy, field electron scanning electron microscopy, impedance spectroscopy and current voltage measurements. There were blue and red shifts of Raman and absorption bands of protoporphyrin+reduced graphene oxide quantum dots nanocomposites, evidencing strong interactions because of π − π electrons stacking, this fact was confirmed by microscopy images showing full coverage of protoporphyrin surface by reduced graphene oxide quantum dots. Electrical characterization of titanium dioxide+protoporphyrin+reduced graphene oxide quantum dots showed one of the highest photo-responses and lowest resistivities, in line with impedance measurements in which these composites presented up to ∼ 200% increase in capacitance, versus
TiO 2
alone. Efficiency of the optimized cell was 6.13 %, this was a value 9.4 % higher versus titanium dioxide+protoporphyrin cell. The reasons for the behavior of optimized cells are discussed based on a bands diagram showing the possibility of a rectifier effect and because of the strong affinity between protoporphyrin and reduced graphene oxide quantum dots.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Dye sensitized solar cells (DSSC) are known for several advantages, they are cheap and flexible and are being developed because of the need of new energy sources. A dye sensitized solar cell is a system capable of converting energy of the light into electric energy. In this type of systems a dye is dispersed on a semiconductor material, the dye absorbs solar energy which is transformed into electricity through a photovoltaic process. When the dye is excited an electron jumps from the dye to the conduction band of the matrix semiconductor. The electrons move through the semiconductor to the electrode and the system closes an external circuit. A redox pair is in contact with the photo-electrode to ensure that the oxidized species of the dye are continuously regenerated in a cyclic process.

Nanotechnology has contributed to the research on DSSC through the use of nanostructured materials of titanium dioxide, zinc oxide or tin oxide as matrix materials. Organic and inorganic dyes have been used as sensitizers. Several compounds based on ruthenium have been used as inorganic sensitizers [1]. On the other hand, squaraines, thiophenes, ullazines, tetrathiafulvalene, porphyrins, imidazoles and graphene quantum dots have been used as organic sensitizers [2–7].

Quantum dots have been proposed as promising candidates for the capture and conservation of solar energy. One of the main advantages it is possible to modify the spectral zone of absorption; that is, because they present quantum confinement effects, it is possible to control the size of the energy gap between the valence and conduction band, besides that, the wavelength of their excitonic peak can be modulated as a function of particle size [8].

Graphene quantum dots (GQD) have been studied intensively, these materials have been fabricated using top-down and bottom-up approaches. Top-down methods used for the fabrication of these materials are hydrothermal, solvothermal, electrochemical stripping, strong acid oxidation and electron bean irradiation. Bottom-up methods are microwave and ultrasonic methods, solvent chemistry methods, and controlled thermal decomposition of aromatic hydrocarbons [9].

Referring to the microwave synthesis of GQD there are some reports at constant power, producing GQD with diameters of 0.5–3.4 nm [10]. Another research showed that adjusting power, temperature and other parameters it was possible to obtain wide size distributions in the range from 1.65 to 21 nm. It has been possible to obtain GQD exhibiting a deep ultraviolet emission of 303 nm (approximately 4.1 eV), which is reported as the shortest emission wavelength of GQD. Most carbohydrates containing C, H and O, can be used as a carbon source to fabricate GQD with hydroxyl and carboxyl groups using this technique [11,12].

There are few reports in the last five years about the synthesis and characterization of reduced graphene oxide quantum dots (RGO-QD). Kong et al. synthesized RGO-QD with different levels of reduction using sodium borohydride as reducing agent, they investigated the photoluminiscence of RGO-QD and found that surface hybridization structure of RGO-QD was attributed to photoluminiscence emission [13]. Zhang et al. synthesized chemically reduced graphene quantum dots using sodium borohydride and demonstrated that the number of carbonyl groups decreased during chemical reduction [14]. Shi et al. synthesized and characterized electrochemically RGO-QD/polypyrrole hybrids and found that the particle size of hybrid compounds depends on the concentration of Py monomer, they also found that the synthesized compounds showed high specific capacitance, rate capability and cyclic stability, these materials might be a promising candidate to be used in energy conversion systems [15]. Xie et al. self-assembled RGO-QD on hematite photo-anode for passiviting surface states in order to use it for water splitting [16]. Most of the research developed up to date focus on chemical or electrochemical synthesis of RGO-QD using sodium borohydride and there are not reports using other reactants.

The significance in this research lies in the first-ever elaboration and characterization of titanium dioxide sensitized solar cells through the use of nanocomposites composed of protoporphyrin IX and microwave-reduced graphene oxide quantum dots. The process involved synthesizing reduced graphene oxide quantum dots via microwave-assisted cutting, which were then combined with protoporphyrin IX before being applied to titanium dioxide. The nanocomposites exhibited strong interactions arising from π − π electron stacking, a fact supported by Raman measurements indicating band shiftings, as well as microscopy images providing visual evidence of complete coverage of protoporphyrin by reduced graphene oxide quantum dots. The electrical characterization of sensitized solar cells revealed high photo-responses and low resistivities, displaying approximately 200 % increase in capacitance compared to titanium dioxide alone. Moreover, the optimized cell achieved an efficiency of 6.13 %, surpassing the titanium dioxide+protoporphyrin cell by 9.4 %. This enhancement could be attributed to rectifier effects and the strong affinity between protoporphyrin and reduced graphene oxide quantum dots. We propose a band diagram illustrating the potential rectifier effect as a contributing factor to the increased efficiency.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of titanium dioxide by sol-gel
ce:section.ce:para:
Titanium dioxide (
TiO 2
) nanoparticles were synthesized and used as matrix material. For the synthesis of
TiO 2
we used titanium isopropoxide ( Ti
C 3
H 6
OH
4
, Sigma-Aldrich, 97 %) as titanium precursor. We added 8 mL of hydrochloric acid (HCL, Merck 37.1 %) to 80 mL of 2-propanol (Sigma-Aldrich, ACS, 99.5 %), after stirring at 350 rpm for 5 min, 8 mL of titanium isopropoxide was added drop-wise at constant stirring, subsequently 2-propanol was added until a volume of 100 mL was completed, next the solution was stirred for half an hour at 250 rpm, after, 3 mL of ammonium hydroxide (
NH 4
OH , Sigma-Aldrich 28 %) was added in order to precipitate the colloid, subsequently the solution was kept stirring for 24 h at 100 rpm, afterwards 2-propanol was evaporated at 90 °C for 24 h, finally the powder was annealed at 400 °C in air for one hour,
TiO 2
powder was used for the elaboration of screen printed films.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of graphene oxide and reduced graphene oxide
ce:section.ce:para:
The procedure for obtaining graphene oxide (GO) was as following: 2.0 g of graphite (20 μm graphite powder, Sigma-Aldrich, 99 %) was taken and dissolved in a mix of 46 mL of sulfuric acid (
H 2
SO 4
, Jalmek, ACS, 95 %) and 1 mL of phosphoric acid (
H 3
PO 4
, Vetec, 85 %), the mixture was placed in a water bath at 4 °C, then 0.3 mol of potassium permanganate (
KMnO 4
, Sigma-Aldrich, ACS, 99 %) were added gradually and the solution was left under stirring for 72 h. Thereafter, 92 mL of distilled water and 100 mL of hydrogen peroxide (Sigma-Aldrich, ACS, 30 wt% en
H 2
O ) were added. Liquids were evaporated and the remaining powder was GO.

To synthesize reduced graphene oxide (RGO) 6.9 ×
10
− 3
moles of ascorbic acid (Golden Bell, USP grade) were mixed with 2 g of GO and dissolved in 410 mL of distilled water, after that, the mixture was sonicated for 2 h, next, it was filtered and washed with distilled water until pH = 7.0, finally the wet powder was dried for 24 h at room temperature (27 °C).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of reduced graphene oxide quantum dots by microwave assisted cutting
ce:section.ce:para:
The synthesis of reduced graphene oxide quantum dots (RGO-QD) was as following: 5 mg of reduced graphene oxide were mixed with 15 mL of ethanol (JT Baker, denatured, 40 %, ACS), and the solution was sonicated for 2 h. The mixture was placed in a G30 wide neck flask and placed in the microwave oven (Anton Paar, Monowave 400), then parameters were adjusted (150 °C for two hours, power of 20 W and pressure of 8.1 bar), after that, it was allowed the system to cool at room temperature (27 °C). Solution coming from microwave oven was purified by filtrating it with a Whatman qualitative filter paper grade 1 (pore size 110
μ m ), after filtering, the liquid was put in an oven at 80 °C for one hour in order to evaporate ethanol, the remaining substance was used as the source of RGO-QD.

RGO-QD was mixed with PIX and/or
TiO 2
in different ways, depending on the purpose, as for example characterization in Raman, UV–Vis, fluorescence spectroscopy, fabrication of photo-electrodes, photo-response measurements, etc. Methods of synthesis of compounds materials are described in the following sections.

Raman spectroscopy was performed in a Thermoscientific brand Raman DXR microscope, using lasers of 633 and 780 nm, slit of 25
μ m and 5
mW /
cm 2
radiation power, most of the times 633 nm laser was used, 780 nm laser was used few times, in order to eliminate the contribution of fluorescence. Ultraviolet visible spectroscopy was carried out in a Shimadzu 3600 unit in the range from 200 to 2000 nm. Fluorescence spectroscopy was done in a Varian Cary Eclipse Fluorescence Spectrophotometer using an excitation wavelength of 260 nm. Dynamic light scattering was done in an Zetasizer Nanoseries SZ90 ZEN 3690 equipment from Malvern Panalytical. RGO-QD + PIX composites were synthesized for Raman and ultraviolet-visible characterizations as following: 0.05 mg of PIX were mixed with 5 mL of N,N-Dimethylformamide (N,N-DMF) (Merck), after 1 mL of this solution was taken and mixed with 1.75 mL of RGO-QD, the mixture was sonicated for 20 min, previous to characterization.

The electrical characterization was done by measuring resistivity in dark and in the light (photo-response) and by measuring photoelectric parameters of the sensitized solar cells. For photo-response measurements, materials were deposited by spin coating at 2000 rpm for 15 s (Vacuum Spin Coater, VTC-100PA) on insulating glass, silver electrodes were painted forming a square shape of 1
cm 2
in order to measure sheet resistance which was multiplied by thickness to calculate resistivity, (see silver electrodes configuration in Fig. 1 ). A voltage of 0.01 V was applied using an automatized 2611A sourcemeter Keithley coupled with a 150 W solar simulator from Oriel-Newport 9600. In all the cases 0.25 mL of liquid were taken from the solutions described in Table 1 and drop-casted on glass, all the solutions were sonicated for 20 min previous to drop-casting.

The solar simulator and power source described in the previous paragraph were used also for solar cell characterization. It was applied a voltage sweep in the range from −1.0 to +1.5 V with a step-wise increase of 0.2 V at a power radiation of 100
mW /
cm 2
. The process of fabrication of photo-electrodes is described in Section 2.6.1.

Impedance spectroscopy was carried out using a potentiostat/galvanostat Autolab model PGSTAT302N potentiostat/galvanostat unit. We worked in a three-electrode electrochemical cell, using four different photo-electrodes: a) FTO/
TiO 2
, b) FTO/
TiO 2
+PIX, c) FTO/
TiO 2
+RGO-QD and d) FTO/
TiO 2
+PIX + RGO-QD. As the auxiliary electrode we used a platinum mesh (52 mesh woven from 0.1 mm, 99.9 %, 2.5 × 2.5 cm), the reference electrode was an Ag/AgCl electrode. Electrodes were immersed in 40 mL of a iodide/triiodide redox electrolyte, the experimental potential redox of the electrolyte was 0.36 V (the value calculated from Nerst equation is 0.4 V). Frequency sweeps were done from 0.1 MHz to 0.1 Hz at 0 V. Impedance data were modeled with equivalent circuits using ZsimpWin software (AMETEK, Scientific instruments) (see the setup for impedance measurements in Fig. 2 ).

The elaboration of the sensitized solar was as following: we used FTO (fluorine doped tin oxide, Sigma-Aldrich, 2.3 mm, sheet resistance of 7 Ω /sq) as substrate for the photo-electrode and counter-electrode. For the photo-electrode, a mixture of 0.5 g of
TiO 2
, 5 drops of Triton X-100 (Sigma-Aldrich) and 15 drops of propylene glycol (Sigma-Aldrich, Mn 400) was mixed and deposited by screen printing using a 120 mesh on a substrate previously cleaned with acetone, the film was annealed in air at 400 °C for 1 h in order to eliminate polymers.

The procedure to synthesize dyes for drop-casting on top of screen printed
TiO 2
was as following: a) PIX. 0.05 mg of PIX were suspended in 5 mL of N,N-DMF, then 0.05 mL of this solution was drop-casted; b) RGO-QD. 0.05 mL of RGO-QD was drop-casted on
TiO 2
; c) PIX-RGO-QD. 0.05 mg of PIX were suspended in 5 mL of N,N-DMF, 0.25 mL of this solution were mixed with 0.8 mL of RGO-QD and sonicated for 20 min, then 0.05 mL of this solution were drop-casted on the top of screen printed
TiO 2
.

For the counter-electrode, 0.5 g of RGO, 5 drops of Triton X-100 and 15 drops of propylene glycol, were mixed, all the system was annealed at 400 °C in air for one hour to eliminate polymers. The cell was sealed with Meltonix (Solaronix 1170–60). Photo-electrode, counter-electrode and Meltonix were gotten together and the assembly was heated with an iron to seal the cell. Finally, the cell was filled with 0.05 mL of electrolyte (see process in Fig. 3 ).

Results of every characterization are presented from the single materials (
TiO 2
, PIX, graphite, GO, RGO or RGO-QD) to the more complex mixtures (composites or compound materials), and after all the results of a characterization are exhausted the next characterization is presented.

Fig. 4 shows ultraviolet visible spectra of carbonaceous materials, i.e. graphite, graphene oxide (GO) and reduced graphene oxide (RGO) this study was done to follow the evolution of the materials in the process to synthesize the last one. Blue spectrum of graphite shows an absorption band at 209 nm that is characteristic of π →
π ∗
transition of the CC aromatic bond, in this spectrum also there is another band at 243 nm that is related with the transition n →
π ∗
of the CC bonds [17]. For the RGO it was observed the shifting of the n →
π ∗
transition to lower energies (243 nm → 249 nm), this is a well-known change that is representative of the reduction of GO, there is also a direct relationship between the shifting and the degree of reduction [17,18]. Results agree with previous reports confirming that we have synthesized RGO.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reduced graphene oxide quantum dots and protoporphyrin IX
ce:section.ce:para:
Fig. 5 shows the ultraviolet visible spectrum of RGO-QD, protoporphyrin IX and the combination of both compounds. The spectrum of RGO-QD shows an absorption around 220 nm which in the same way that in Fig. 4 is related to the transition π −
π ∗
of the links sp2 in the bond CC, indicating a π conjugated micro-structure, while the 248 nm band it is associated with the n −
π ∗
which is attributed to CO bonds. According to previous reports on graphene quantum dots, the position of the principal absorption band is known to be associated with the size of the quantum dot. Specifically, bands observed at 380 nm, 500 nm, and 600 nm correspond to quantum dots containing 19, 48, and 79 conjugated carbon rings, respectively. Therefore, based on our band at 280 nm, it suggests the presence of reduced graphene quantum dots with dimensions of a few nanometers [19].

The spectrum of the PIX shows the characteristic bands of porphyrins: the Soret band at 405 nm and four Q bands (505, 439, 576 and 632 nm) [20]. The spectrum when both materials are combined shows interesting contributions from both materials, first, the strong absorption related with the Soret band but with a small blue-shift (hypsochromic shift), second, Q bands are shifted to lower energies (bathochromic shift), both bathochromic and hypsochromic shifts are indicative of strong molecular interactions between both materials [20].

Photo-electrodes were elaborated and characterized before using them (see Fig. 6 ). When RGO-QD was mixed with
TiO 2
the principal change was an increase in the absorbance, but the transitions ( n −
π ∗
or π −
π ∗
) were kept at the same wavelength. The most interesting point in this figure is the change as
TiO 2
was mixed with PIX and/or RGO-QD. The spectrum of
TiO 2
is characteristic of a semiconductor with the absorption threshold beginning at ∼ 400 nm and reaching a plateau at ∼ 300 nm, but when it was combined with PIX the threshold shifted up to ∼ 550 nm reaching the higher absorbance plateau at ∼ 450 nm. When RGO-QD were added (material called Mix) the principal change was a small shift to lower energies. These results suggest that adding PIX would increase the efficiency of the sensitized cell, but the addition of RGO-QD would help to a lesser extent.

Fig. 7 shows Tauc plots for band gap calculation. The band gap for
TiO 2
was similar to values reported in previous studies [21]. When
TiO 2
was combined with PIX the band gap decreased a little and increased as
TiO 2
was combined with RGO-QD, it could be because of the higher value of the band of RGO-QD. Finally, for the combination of the three materials there was an important decrease, suggesting that this will be a good material for photoelectric applications.

Fig. 8 shows the fluorescence of RGO-QD in the range from 400 to 650 nm. The sample was excited at 260 nm that is the threshold absorption of RGO-QD. The sample showed a maximum emission at 452 nm that it is related with a blue fluorescence as it is shown in Fig. 9.

This spectrum as well as the absorbance spectrum of RGO-QD reported in Fig. 6 are similar to the ones reported by Singh et al. and confirm that we have synthesized RGO-QD with quantum effect, and not small RGO, as RGO do not show fluorescence [22,23].

Fig. 10 shows the Raman spectra of graphite, graphene oxide and reduced graphene oxide. The G band appears at ∼ 1580
cm
− 1
, and is related to the first order dispersion of the
E
2 g
of carbon atoms with
sp 2
bonds [24]. The band D at 1350
cm
− 1
is related to the presence of defects [25]. The band at ∼ 2660
cm
− 1
corresponds to an overtone of the D band and is known as the 2D band [26]. Likewise, after oxidizing the graphite, the G peak widened while the intensity of the D band increased, this increase is attributed to the decrease in the size of the
sp 2
domains in the graphene, which is associated with the presence of oxygenated functional groups, that is, graphite became graphene oxide [27]. An interesting point was the shifting of the G band (graphite, 1578
cm
− 1
→ GO, 1594
cm
− 1
→ RGO, 1593
cm
− 1
), this phenomenon is related to the presence of isolated double bonds in GO and in RGO [26]. The parameter defect density was calculated. This value represents the ratio of intensities between band D and band G
I D
/
I G
. An increase in this ratio suggests a decrease in the average size of
sp 2
domains, possibly indicating the formation of new graphitic domains that are smaller in size but more abundant in number (see Table 2 ) [26,28–31]. In general, Raman spectroscopy is not a reliable method to determine the
sp 3
content of a material. While there are specific conditions under which one may deduce the
sp 3
content, these conditions are not related to the experimental parameters used in this study [32].

Fig. 11 shows Raman spectrum of RGO-QD as synthesized from the microwave oven (RGO-QD + ethanol, blue line). After most of ethanol was evaporated a clear spectra of a carbonaceous material with its characteristic G and D bands (red line) was revealed, this means that a high level of purity was reached.

The defect density values of RGO-QD + ethanol and RGO-QD are presented in Table 2 to facilitate a comparison with values of graphite, GO, and RGO. Notably, the defect density values of RGO-QD are observed to be the highest among these materials. A previous report on graphene quantum dots has indeed established correlations between domain size and several parameters, including the ratio of
I D
/
I G
, the value of full width half maximum, or the frequency of G and D bands. However, it is important to acknowledge that the experimental conditions employed in this current study differ to some extent from those in the aforementioned report. Consequently, these correlations cannot be directly applied to the findings of our research. The most probable reason for the increase in
I D
/
I G
values could be attributed to the rise in the number of smaller
sp 2
domains [19,28,32].

Fig. 12 shows Raman spectroscopy of RGO-QD using two different excitation sources (633 nm (1.95 eV) and 780 nm (1.59 eV)). When molecules are excited reach two states of energy, virtual and real, the real one is when an excited electronic state (fluorescence) occurs. This process depends on the excitation energy of the laser, fluorescence interference can occur from the analyzed material itself or from impurities. Higher energy lasers (514 and 633 nm), can produce a background fluorescence, while wavelengths within the near-infrared (NIR) (780 and 1064 nm) show lower levels of fluorescence or no fluorescence because their energy may not be enough to excite the molecules [33]. The important information from Fig. 12 is that despite of withdrawing the possible contribution of fluorescence using 780 nm laser, is still evident the presence of a carbonaceous material with bands D and G.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reduced graphene oxide quantum dots combined with protoporphyrin IX
ce:section.ce:para:
It is important to follow any change in the route to synthesize photo-electrode materials with the combination
TiO 2
+RGO-QD + PIX, therefore we did Raman analysis of the combination RGO-QD + PIX (see Fig. 13 ). The principal information in this figure is: 1) the spectrum of the combined material present contributions of both RGO-QD and PIX, which is evidenced by a small D and G bands, and 2) the fact that this spectrum is similar to the one of PIX but shifted to higher energies, both evidences are indicative of a stronger connection between both materials. This result agrees well with the result of ultraviolet-visible spectroscopy (see Fig. 5) that showed strong changes as both materials were combined.

Fig. 14 shows Raman spectra of photo-electrodes. We can see the characteristic spectrum of the protoporphyrin IX with the porphyrin band at 1550
cm
− 1
. In the spectrum of titanium dioxide we can see four active modes (148, 397, 525 and 638
cm
− 1
), these values are close to those presented in the literature for the anatase phase [34,35]. Anatase is tetragonal and belongs to the space group
D
4 h
19
I 4 / amd
. The primitive unit cell contains two
TiO 2
chemical units and has 15 optical modes but only three modes are active (
A
1 g
,
B
1 g
and
E g
) [35]. The spectrum of
TiO 2
+PIX (blue line) keep the four active modes of
TiO 2
and presents a hypsochromic shift (to higher energies) of the principal band of PIX, this is an evidence of the interaction at molecular level between both materials. The spectrum of
TiO 2
+RGO-QD (purple line) is interesting as it keeps some of the characteristic bands of both materials, specifically bands
E g
of
TiO 2
and bands D and G of RGO-QD, however it seems that there is the formation of new bands in the range from 400 to 700
cm
− 1
, another possibility could be a blue-shift of
TiO 2
bands, either way, the conclusion is that there is a strong interaction between both compounds even stronger compared with the interaction between
TiO 2
and PIX, to the point that some
TiO 2
bands are modified. Finally the olive color spectrum belongs to the material called Mix, this is a combination of the three materials:
TiO 2
, PIX and RGO-QD, it is noteworthy that some of the original modes were kept (
TiO 2
at 148
cm
− 1
and bands D and G of RGO-QD), however another bands of
TiO 2
and of PIX were shifted, all this characteristics suggest that while some of the initial properties of the materials were kept, new characteristics and strong interactions among components emerged.

Fig. 15 shows AFM images (topography and phase contrast) of RGO-QD. To the extent of the capabilities of the equipment there seems to be a kind of not well defined bimodal distribution (80 and 185 nm) with higher number of particles near to 80 nm and a Lorentzian fitting with representative size of 138 nm. It is noteworthy the well-rounded forms of the RGO-QD particles. Fig. 15 d) and e) show profilometry of a smaller RGO-QD.

Size and zeta potential ( ζ ) measurements of RGO-QD suspended in ethanol were performed (Fig. 16 ), and we found an hydrodynamic size of 142 nm, this value was in accordance with AFM measurements. Referring to ζ measurements the value was of −0.3 mV, this value was far away of the stability region of ± 30 mV. The higher unstability of the solution could be the reason behind the broad distribution of sizes shown in Fig. 15c), i.e. RGO particles were coalescing at a higher rate resulting in a high variety of sizes.

FESEM images of GO, RGO and RGO-QD are shown in Fig. 17 . Fig. 17a) and b) are images of GO, we can see big and wrinkled sheets of GO, the main difference with RGO (Fig. 17c)) is that in the latter one we had smaller sheets, in some cases less than 2
μ m long. Fig. 17d) shows images of RGO-QD these particles have well rounded forms and it is remarkable the contrast when they are compared with RGO or GO. The histogram shows a representative size of ∼ 130 nm, this value correlates well with AFM and DLS measurements.

The image of
TiO 2
nanoparticles synthesized by sol-gel is presented in Fig. 18 , we can see that most of the particles have a uniform size. The histogram shows a narrow and left modal distribution with particles in the range from 12 to 20 nm, with a representative size of 16.5 nm.

Fig. 19 shows the combination of PIX with RGO-QD. Image a) is PIX powder in flakes presentation, figures b) an c) are of PIX previously dissolved in N,N-DMF and at different magnifications. It is interesting that when PIX was dissolved in N,N-DMF and dropped on a glass substrate, curious shapes like flowers or donuts were formed. Subsequently, both materials were combined and it was noteworthy that there was a lot of RGO-QD tightly attached to PIX, this is a good thing because this strong interaction serves to ensure a good connection between both materials in order to obtain a synergic behavior, such as a faster transfer of charge. This image confirm our previous statements in the explanations of Figs. 5 (ultraviolet visible spectroscopy) and 13 (Raman spectroscopy) in the sense that higher shifts suggested strong interactions between the two compounds.

Nanocomposites of
TiO 2
and PIX were analyzed and are presented in Fig. 20 . There are some
TiO 2
nanoparticles attached to PIX, an interesting point here is that in addition to the attaching there is a kind of thin film covering nanoparticles. Fig. 20c) is a zoom of Fig. 20b) and it was processed to invert colors, therefore it was possible to see several nanoparticles covered with the film, some of them are pointed by arrows. Another important point is that there was not the same affinity between these materials if we compare it with the strong interactions of PIX + RGO-QD, the lower interaction is evidenced by some areas of PIX that are not covered with
TiO 2
nanoparticles. This could be because of the organic-inorganic interactions, in contrast to organic-organic bonding between PIX and RGO-QD.

Finally, Fig. 21 shows images of the compound
TiO 2
+PIX + RGO-QD. Most of the surface was covered by
TiO 2
nanoparticles, however, it seems that between
TiO 2
and PIX there was a kind of inter-phase of RGO-QD (Fig. 21a)), i.e. RGO-QD are more attracted to PIX than
TiO 2
, once that PIX and RGO-QD were attached, most of the surface was covered with
TiO 2
nanoparticles. Fig. 21c) is a zoom of figure b) where RGO-QD (bigger particles) and
TiO 2
(smaller particles) can be seen.

Summarizing, from AFM, DLS and FESEM there were evidences which show that we have synthesized RGO-QD in the order of ∼ 100 nm. We also have synthesized
TiO 2
nanoparticles in order to use them as a matrix. It was demonstrated that RGO-QD and PIX had higher affinity, while there was lower affinity between PIX and
TiO 2
. When PIX,
TiO 2
and RGO-QD were combined there was strong interactions between PIX and RGO-QD in the background and after
TiO 2
nanoparticles covered most of the available surface.

Impedance spectroscopy was done with the purpose of obtaining some kind of resolution in the frequency according to the bricklayer model [36]. Fig. 22 shows the Nyquist plots of representative materials and Table 3 shows the results of the fitting with equivalent circuits (
χ 2
<
10
− 3
). We obtained three semicircles in every case, however we have isolated the central one because in agreement with the bricklayer model and with the values calculated from the fitting (red line) this is the semicircle related with our materials (
TiO 2
,
TiO 2
+PIX,
TiO 2
+RGO-QD or
TiO 2
+RGO-QD + PIX). The fitting was done with the equivalent circuit (RQ)(RQ) and true capacitance values were computed from eq. 1 [37].
(1)
C =
Q 0
× R
1 / n
R

There were two facts, the first is the reduction of the resistance by one and a half and one fourth when
TiO 2
was mixed with PIX or RGO-QD respectively, while that when
TiO 2
was mixed with PIX + RGO-QD there was a reduction by one third, this change suggest that there is a kind of percolation inside
TiO 2
, with PIX molecules or RGO-QD working as connectors or bridges between
TiO 2
nanoparticles. The second fact is an important increase in the capacitance up to ∼ 200%, when
TiO 2
, RGO-QD and PIX were mixed, in contrast to
TiO 2
+PIX or
TiO 2
+RGO-QD, this increase indicates that there is a synergic connection when the three materials were mixed.

The bricklayer model states that every RQ sub-circuit could be ascribed to grain boundaries or any other boundary in the system, based on the values of capacitance shown in Table 3 we could assume that the sub-circuit RQ that we are using for the fitting is describing the electrical behavior of
TiO 2
or any other of the compound materials that we used as photo-electrodes.

Table 4 shows the sheet resistance of some materials used in this research. The value for
TiO 2
is characteristic of a very resistive material, on the other side, is remarkable the lower value of carbon materials, above all the resistance of RGO-QD which is the lowest one [38].

Fig. 23 shows the behavior of resistivity in darkness an under illumination of 100
mW /
cm 2
. Table 5 shows resistivities in dark and under illumination as well as photo-sensitivity, the latter parameter was calculated according to eq. 2,
(2)
Ph =
R D
−
R L
R L
where•
P h
Photosensitivity•
R D
Resistivity in dark•
R L
Resistivity in light

There was a high consistency in these results. The material with the highest resistivity in darkness was
TiO 2
( 6.2 ×
10 6
Ω .cm), and the lowest resistivity was for RGO-QD ( 2.8 ×
10 4
Ω .cm). When
TiO 2
was mixed with PIX or RGO-QD the resistivity was reduced by half and by one third respectively, in accordance with impedance measurements. It seemed to be that neither PIX nor RGO-QD mixed with
TiO 2
reached a higher decrease of the resistivity, however it was noteworthy that when both RGO-QD and PIX were mixed with
TiO 2
, resistivity dropped to only 0.8 % of the initial value, again in accordance with the highest increase in capacitance detected by impedance spectroscopy, i.e. there was the highest concentration of available charge carriers. Another important information is that in spite of the low resistivity in the dark, a higher photo-sensitivity was kept. All this information suggest that we have succeeded in fabricating a synergic material mixing PIX and RGO-QD an using this combined material as sensitizer in a matrix of nanosized
TiO 2
.

Three configurations of cells were tested: a) TiO
2
∣ electrolyte ∣ RGO, b)
TiO 2
+PIX ∣ electrolyte ∣ RGO, c)
TiO 2
+PIX + RGO ∣ electrolyte ∣ RGO. J-V plots are presented in Fig. 24 and the comparison of the photoelectric parameters is presented in Table 6 . As was expected there was a high increase in the efficiency when
TiO 2
was mixed with PIX, and there was a modest but significant increase when RGO-QD was added (+9.4 % versus
TiO 2
+PIX). The increase in the efficiency is in line with ultraviolet spectroscopy (see Fig. 6), and agrees in some extent with other characterizations as photo-response or impedance spectroscopy, however we have to continue the research studying the mechanisms behind all these phenomena in order to reach higher efficiencies.

The present research has been inspired in previous reports about the compatibility between RGO and porphyrins [39–41]. There are reports about a strong affinity between porphyrins and reduced graphene oxide in solution and in films, it has been found that this type of interactions produce red-shift in the porphyrins absorption band as we observed in this research. Another interesting topic is the non-covalent functionalization of graphene, with planar organic molecules via π − π stacking, Van der Waals, or electrostatic forces. Another researchers describe systems of
TiO 2
-RGO systems where two dimensional graphene can accept and shuttle electrons [42]. An evidence of interactions between this type of materials is the intramolecular energy transfer confirmed by the quenching of the fluorescence [43].

In our case the PIX molecule (see Fig. 25 ) is a macro-cycle with four pyrroles. In addition to the double bonds of pyrroles there are another double bonds, connecting the pyrroles and in the oxygenated groups, that is, there are π bonds and consequently delocalized π electrons. Referring to the compound material Mix (PIX + RGO-QD), it is easy to understand the strong affinity that we observed given that RGO-QD also has π electrons. In this way the mechanism for the interaction between RGO-QD and porphyrin could be the π − π stacking phenomena.

In electrical characterizations
TiO 2
+PIX + RGO-QD and PIX + RGO-QD presented similar electrical properties, i.e.
TiO 2
worked only as inert matrix material. In impedance spectroscopy we observed that the compound
TiO 2
+PIX + RGO-QD presented the highest capacitance and lower resistance. Additionally, this compound showed higher photo-response and low resistivity. All this information evidenced that we synthesized a material with higher electrical interconnections and the role of RGO-QD could be to inject electrons to the nanocomposite and to serve as a path for electrical conduction.

Referring to the performance of the sensitized cells, we have elaborated the bands diagram of the system
TiO 2
+PIX + RGO-QD, taking into account values from our measurements and from previous researches (Fig. 26 ) [44–49]. This diagram suggest that besides the characteristics described above, there could a rectifier effect, as the conduction band of RGO-QD is lower than the LUMO of PIX. This configuration helps to separate charge carriers, and combined with more conductive photo-electrodes, resulted in higher efficiency.

In this research we have fabricated for the first time a sensitized solar cell using as sensitizer a compound based on PIX + RGO-QD. Another novelty was that RGO-QD were synthesized by microwave assisted cutting, obtaining a broad distribution of sizes. When RGO-QD and PIX were combined underwent strong interactions at supramolecular level to the point that RGO-QD covered most of the surface of PIX. On the contrary,
TiO 2
nanoparticles and PIX showed less affinity. The increase in the efficiency of the cell when PIX + RGO-QD was used as sensitizer was modest, however we found that this compound had the highest capacitance and photo-response as well as lower resistivity, these results suggest that if experimental conditions are adequately handled it could be possible to increase the efficiency even more.

P.I. Lopez-Picazo: Investigation, Methodology, Writing – original draft. M. Sanchez-Tizapa: Investigation, Writing – review & editing. M.M. Tostado-Plascencia: Investigation, Writing – review & editing. M. Flores-Martínez: Writing – review & editing. M.A. Carreón-Álvarez: Investigation, Writing – review & editing. R. Castañeda-Valderrama: Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: M. Sanchez-Tizapa reports financial support was provided by University of Guadalajara. P.I. Lopez-Picazo reports a relationship with Consejo Nacional de Humanidades, Ciencia y Tecnologia de México, CONACYT that includes: scholarship for Ph.D studies.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors acknowledge to Consejo Nacional de Ciencia y Tecnología (CONACyT) of Mexico for the PhD scholarship of P.I. Lopez-Picazo, to the Laboratorio de Medio Ambiente y Energías Renovables by allowing us the use of its facilities, and to Programa de Fortalecimiento de la Investigación y el Posgrado of the Universidad de Guadalajara.

nanoparticles

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Reduced graphene oxide and porphyrin. an interactive affair in 2-d
ACS Nano
